results.possibleIdentity: Lawrence Hahn was a research affiliate and artist-architect associated with the MIT Center for Advanced Visual Studies (CAVS) between 1970 and 1972. Born on May 9, 1949, his practice during this period focused on architecture and the intersection of visual studies with technology. CAVS, founded by György Kepes, was a prominent hub for collaborations between artists, scientists, and engineers, sharing many goals and collaborators with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization during the late 1960s and early 1970s.

Larry Hahn is a dancer, set designer, and builder whose career in the performing arts spans over five decades, beginning in the early 1970s. He is known for his work with several major choreographers, including Doug Varone and Senta Driver. In the context of art and technology, he collaborated with Robert Wechsler and Frieder Weiss (founders of the Palindrome Inter-Media Performance Group) on projects involving interactive dance and motion-sensing technology, such as the 'EyeCon' system. He was honored with a New York Dance and Performance Award (Bessie) in 1996 for his sustained achievements in the field.

results.possibleIdentity: Jill Rosenberg was a project director for Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization founded in 1966 by Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg to foster collaborations between artists and engineers. Active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, she served as the Project Director for E.A.T.'s Technical Services Program, which was a core matching service that paired artists with technical experts for their creative projects. Her name appears in the credits of major E.A.T. publications and projects from this era, including the 'Pavilion' project for Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan. She is acknowledged as an influential figure in discussions regarding the intersection of art and technology during that period.

Jill Nassau Rosenberg is an artist who began her career as a graphic artist and illustrator in the late 1960s. During the 1960s and 1970s, she worked for various advertising agencies and established her own graphic art business, which she operated until 1990. In the early 1980s, she transitioned into fine art, focusing on large-scale representational oil portraits and later exploring different media and techniques, including Fauvistic landscapes and sculpture. She received her Bachelor of Arts from Maryville University in St. Louis and has participated in numerous solo and group exhibitions since the mid-1980s.

Jill Rosenberg was an active participant in the New York City arts and music scene in the early 1960s. She attended the Fashion Institute of Technology (FIT) in 1962, where she was known for her involvement in the Greenwich Village folk music culture. She is noted for introducing contemporaries to prominent local venues such as 'The Cat' (The Fat Black Pussycat) on MacDougal Street, a central hub for the folk music revival that included figures like Bob Dylan and Joni Mitchell.
